speaker: ALEX
text: This is Git Going with GitHub, episode 18: Glossary of Terms. I am Alex. By the end of this episode, Glossary of Terms should feel less like a wall of GitHub words and more like a set of moves you can trust.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I am here for the practical questions: what should I listen for, what can go wrong, and what is the next calm move?

speaker: ALEX
text: Today we are working on this: Comprehensive glossary: Git, GitHub, open source, and accessibility terminology. I want the learner to leave with a mental map, not just a remembered path through buttons.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the goal is understanding first, then action, then confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. We are building a rhythm: orient yourself, take one intentional action, then verify what changed before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: Every Term You Need for Open Source Contribution: This glossary is your reference for the entire two-day workshop. The next useful detail is concrete: When you hear a term you don't recognize - check here first.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Learning Cards: Navigating the Glossary has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. Press H to jump between term headings (h3 level) within each category section. Use the Elements List (NVDA+F7 or Insert+F6) to see all terms at once and type a few letters to filter. Jump to "Alphabetical Quick Reference" at the bottom for a flat A-Z lookup table. Each term is a bold h3 heading -- increase zoom and scan headings to browse terms quickly. The alphabetical table at the bottom works well at high zoom since each row is self-contained. Use Ctrl+F in your browser to search for any term by name.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Repository (Repo): a repository is the container for an entire project. That matters in practice: It holds all the project's files, folders, documentation, and the complete history of every change ever made.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Anchor this part on Organization (Org). An organization is a group account on GitHub that multiple people can belong to. This is the part to say slowly: Open source projects typically live inside an organization rather than under a personal account.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: a fork is a personal copy of someone else's repository, living in your own GitHub account. The listener should be able to check this: When you fork a repo, you get all its files and history.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. Do not treat Remote as decoration. A remote is a connection to a repository hosted elsewhere (usually on GitHub). That is not trivia. Your local Git repository can have multiple remotes. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. origin - your fork on GitHub. upstream - the original repository you forked from.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the safe way to learn from that example?

speaker: ALEX
text: To see your remotes has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git remote -v. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should happen before anyone copies and runs it?

speaker: ALEX
text: To add a remote has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git remote add upstream https://github.com/original-owner/repo.git. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. origin is the default name Git gives to the remote repository you cloned from. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: When you clone your fork, origin points to your fork on GitHub.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before the learner moves on. This part earns its place because.gitignore is a special file in the root of your repository that tells Git which files or folders to ignore - meaning Git will not track or commit them. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: Why use.gitignore? has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: If someone is taking notes, this is the short list. Prevent committing temporary files (.DS Store, Thumbs.db). Ignore build outputs (dist/, build/, node modules/). Keep secrets out of Git (.env files, API keys, credentials). Avoid committing IDE-specific files (.vscode/,.idea/).

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should they understand before typing anything?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the learner-facing version.gitignore only ignores untracked files. Put another way, if you already committed a file, you must remove it from Git's tracking first.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git rm --cached filename. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you keep commands from becoming magic words?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Clone: cloning copies a repository from GitHub to your local computer so you can work with it in VS Code or your preferred editor. That matters in practice: When you clone, you get all the files and the complete history.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git clone https://github.com/your-username/repo-name.git. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Anchor this part on Branch. A branch is a separate line of development inside a repository. This is the part to say slowly: The main branch (often called main or master) holds the stable, released version of the project. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid Git habit is to know which branch you are on, what changed, and what confirmation you expect before you run the next command.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: Common branch names has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. main - the primary, stable branch. develop - integration branch (not all projects have this). feature/my-new-thing - convention for feature branches. fix/broken-button - convention for bug fix branches. docs/update-readme - convention for documentation-only branches.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Commit. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat Commit as decoration. A commit is a saved snapshot of your changes at a moment in time. That is not trivia. Good commit messages are in the imperative mood: "Fix typo in README" not "Fixed typo" or "Fixing typo.".

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. A message describing what changed and why. A unique SHA hash (a fingerprint like a1b2c3d). The author and timestamp. The changes (additions and deletions to files).

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Fix broken link in accessibility guide; The link to the NVDA download page was using an outdated URL.; Updated to the current direct download page.; Fixes 42. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Diff. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Diff is still useful because a diff (short for difference) shows what changed between two versions of a file. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Lines that were added are shown in green (with a + prefix).

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: ALEX
text: Hold that next to this. Put Issue into plain language. An issue is a discussion item in a GitHub repository. The useful version is: Every issue gets a sequential number (like 42) and can have labels, assignees, milestones, and comments. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. Reporting bugs. Requesting features or improvements. Asking questions. Discussing ideas.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid issue habit is to read the title, the body, and the timeline before acting. You are listening for the requested action, the missing evidence, and the person who needs a response.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Pull Request (PR). What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. A pull request is a proposal to merge changes from one branch into another. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: When you have finished working on your fork or feature branch, you open a PR to say "here is my work - please review it and consider merging it.".

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. Which branch you want to merge into which target branch. The diff (all changes you made). A description of what you changed and why. Discussion threads and reviews from others.

speaker: ALEX
text: That connects to another useful point. This part earns its place because code review is the process of one or more collaborators reading and providing feedback on a PR before it is merged. That gives the learner a foothold: good code reviews are kind, specific, and constructive.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. Comment - leave a note on a line (not a formal verdict). Approve - signal that they are happy with the changes. Request Changes - indicate that specific things need to be addressed before merging.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Merge. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Merge: Merging is combining changes from one branch into another. The next useful detail is concrete: When a PR is approved, a maintainer merges it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Here is the learner-facing version. A merge conflict happens when two branches have both changed the same part of the same file in different ways. Put another way, Git doesn't know which version to keep, so it pauses and asks you to resolve it manually. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Upstream. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Upstream: upstream refers to the original repository that you forked from. That matters in practice: When the upstream project has new changes that you want to bring into your fork, you "sync" your fork with upstream.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Anchor this part on Label. Labels are colored tags applied to issues and PRs to categorize them.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. bug - something isn't working. enhancement - new feature or request. documentation - documentation-only change. good first issue - good for newcomers (look for this when finding your first contribution!). help wanted - maintainers want community help. accessibility - accessibility-related.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid project habit is to treat metadata as decision support. Labels, status, assignees, and notifications tell you what kind of attention the work needs.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Milestone. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: a milestone groups issues and PRs toward a goal or deadline. The listener should be able to check this: For example: "v2.0 Release" or "Hackathon Day 1." Milestones show progress as a percentage of closed versus open items.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. Do not treat Project (GitHub Projects) as decoration. A GitHub Project is a flexible board for tracking work. That is not trivia. It can show issues and PRs in table view, board view (kanban), or roadmap view. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Maintainer. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Maintainer is still useful because a maintainer is someone with write or admin access to a repository. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Maintainers review PRs, triage issues, manage releases, and make decisions about the project's direction.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. Put Contributor into plain language. Anyone who contributes to an open source project is a contributor. The useful version is: Contributing includes code, documentation, design, testing, translation, issue triage, and community support.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Triage. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Triaging issues means reviewing new issues to categorize them, add labels, assign them, close duplicates, and communicate with the reporter. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Triage is an important contribution that doesn't require coding skills.

speaker: ALEX
text: Put that beside the next piece. This part earns its place because a collaborator is someone who has been explicitly granted write access to a repository. That gives the learner a foothold: collaborators can commit directly to the repo without forking (though good practice is still to use branches and PRs). That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Part 4: Common Abbreviations and Slang. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Common Abbreviations and Slang: These abbreviations appear frequently in GitHub comments, PR descriptions, and commit messages.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on HEAD. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the learner-facing version. HEAD is Git's way of saying "where you are right now." It's a pointer to the current commit on the current branch. Put another way, you'll see HEAD referenced in commands like.

speaker: ALEX
text: A few details make that real. When you're on main, HEAD points to the latest commit on main. When you switch branches, HEAD moves to that branch. HEAD^ or HEAD 1 means "the commit before HEAD".

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git reset HEAD 1 Undo the last commit; git diff HEAD Show uncommitted changes. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Detached HEAD. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Detached HEAD: a "detached HEAD" occurs when you check out a specific commit instead of a branch. That matters in practice: You're no longer "on" a branch - just looking at a snapshot in time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the part I would want someone to say out loud while they work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A learner should always know what they are trying to prove before they take the next action.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Why it happens. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Why it happens has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git checkout a1b2c3d Checking out a commit directly. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on What this means. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: What this means has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. Any commits you make won't belong to any branch. If you switch branches, those commits become "orphaned".

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to fix it. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat How to fix it as decoration. If you made commits in detached HEAD state and want to keep them. That is not trivia. Git will warn you verbosely when entering detached HEAD state.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git branch new-branch-name Creates a branch from your current position; git checkout new-branch-name. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Stash. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Stash is still useful because git stash temporarily saves your uncommitted changes and reverts your working directory to a clean state. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: It's useful when you need to switch branches but aren't ready to commit.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Common workflow. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Common workflow has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like You're working on branch A; git stash Save your changes temporarily; git checkout main Switch to main; Do something on main.; git checkout branch-a Switch back; git stash pop Restore your changes. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is less about memorizing and more about noticing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Once the learner can name the move, the interface becomes much less intimidating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What stays the same when the tool changes?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Use case: "I need to quickly switch branches to check something, but I'm in the middle of work I'm not ready to commit.".

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. git stash - save changes. git stash list - see what's stashed. git stash pop - restore and remove from stash. git stash apply - restore but keep in stash. git stash drop - delete a stash.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. This part earns its place because rebasing is an alternative to merging. That gives the learner a foothold: instead of creating a merge commit, rebase moves (replays) your commits on top of another branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on When to use rebase. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: When to use rebase has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: That becomes easier when you listen for these cues. Keep a linear project history. Clean up your branch before submitting a PR. Incorporate upstream changes into your feature branch.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. When NOT to rebase has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. Shared branches (never rebase main or public branches). After pushing to GitHub (unless you're comfortable force pushing).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Basic rebase workflow. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Basic rebase workflow: rebase conflicts are resolved the same way as merge conflicts (edit file, remove markers, git add, git rebase --continue).

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git checkout feature-branch; git rebase main; Fix any conflicts; git push --force-with-lease See Force Push below. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Cherry-Pick. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on Cherry-Pick. Cherry-picking applies a single commit from one branch onto another. This is the part to say slowly: Use case: You made a bug fix on the wrong branch and want to apply it to main without merging the whole branch.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git checkout main; git cherry-pick a1b2c3d Apply commit a1b2c3d to main. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on When to use. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: warning: Cherry-picking duplicates commits (creates a new commit with the same changes). The listener should be able to check this: Avoid cherry-picking commits that are part of an active feature branch - it causes confusion.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the pieces that turn the idea into a usable move. Extract a single fix from a larger feature branch. Backport a bug fix to an older release branch. Undo a commit on one branch but keep it on another.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Fetch vs Pull. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat Fetch vs Pull as decoration. git fetch downloads changes from a remote repository but doesn't merge them into your current branch. That is not trivia. It updates your local copy of remote branches (like origin/main or upstream/main) without touching your working files. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git fetch origin Download updates from origin. git pull origin main Fetch + merge origin/main into current branch. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on When to use fetch. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: When to use fetch has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. You want to see what changed without committing to merging. You want to review changes before integrating them. You're syncing upstream and want to inspect first.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now shift from knowing the term to using it. Put When to use pull into plain language. Best practice for collaboration: Use fetch first, review with git log origin/main, then decide to merge or rebase.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. You know you want the latest changes merged immediately. You're working alone on a branch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Force Push. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Force pushing (git push --force or git push -f) overwrites the remote branch with your local version, replacing its history. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: This is dangerous because it can delete commits that others have based work on.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. When force push is okay has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Your own feature branch that no one else is working on. After rebasing to clean up history before merging a PR.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on When force push is NEVER okay. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: When force push is NEVER okay has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. The main branch. Shared branches where others have based work on your commits. Public branches with collaborators.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Safer alternative: --force-with-lease. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the learner-facing version. --force-with-lease only force pushes if no one else has pushed to the remote branch since you last fetched. Put another way, if someone pushed changes, the command fails and warns you.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git push --force-with-lease origin feature-branch. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Why force push is needed after rebase. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Why force push is needed after rebase: when you rebase, Git rewrites commit history. That matters in practice: The remote branch and your local branch now have conflicting histories.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where confidence starts to build. Anchor this part on SHA / Hash. Every commit, file, and tree in Git is identified by a SHA hash - a 40-character string of letters and numbers (e.g., a1b2c3d4e5f6.). This is the part to say slowly: the learner will see shortened versions like a1b2c3d in the GitHub UI. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Tag / Release. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: a tag marks a specific commit as significant - usually a version release like v1.0.0. The listener should be able to check this: Tags are permanent references (unlike branches, which move with each new commit).

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Do not treat Actions / Workflow / CI/CD as decoration. GitHub Actions is an automation platform built into GitHub. That is not trivia. Workflows are automated scripts (written in YAML) that run in response to events - like a PR being opened or code being pushed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Status Check. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Status Check is still useful because a status check is the result of an automated test or workflow run on a PR. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Maintainers often require status checks to pass before merging.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Green checkmark - all checks passed. Red X - one or more checks failed. Yellow dot - checks are still running.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put Webhook into plain language. A webhook is an automated notification that GitHub sends to another service when something happens (a push, a PR opened, etc.). The useful version is: Webhooks power integrations with tools like Slack, project management systems, and CI services. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on GitHub Discussions. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Discussions are a threaded forum built into GitHub, separate from Issues. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: They are used for open-ended conversation, Q&A, and community announcements.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because your GitHub profile is your public identity. That gives the learner a foothold: it shows your name, bio, location, repositories, contribution activity (the green squares), and pinned projects.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on GitHub Copilot. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub Copilot: GitHub Copilot is an AI-powered coding assistant. The next useful detail is concrete: It suggests code, documentation, and commit messages.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. Every healthy open source project has these files in the root of the repository. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Learning Cards: Alphabetical Quick Reference. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Learning Cards: Alphabetical Quick Reference has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Press T to jump to the table, then use Ctrl+Alt+Arrow keys to navigate rows and columns. Each row has two cells: Term and Definition -- column headers are announced on first entry. For longer definitions, the table keeps them concise; see the full entry above for details. This table is designed for quick lookups -- each row fits on a single line at most zoom levels. If the table wraps awkwardly, try reducing zoom slightly or switching to a wider window. Bold term names in the left column create a visible scan line.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on Alphabetical Quick Reference. Next: Appendix B: Screen Reader Cheat Sheet Teaching chapter: Chapter 02: Understanding GitHub.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 18. Next in the series is episode 19, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
